title: M S Vishwanatha Rao S/O Late M Shankar Rao vs M S Nagaraj Since Dead By Lrs Nagaratna ... on 30 April, 2015
body: M S Vishwanatha Rao S/O Late M Shankar Rao vs M S Nagaraj Since Dead By Lrs Nagaratna ... on 30 April, 2015 Author: A.V.Chandrashekara Bench: A.V.Chandrashekara 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU DATED THIS THE 30TH DAY OF APRIL, 2015 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE A.V.CHANDRASHEKARA R.S.A. NO.238/2005 BETWEEN: M S VISHWANATHA RAO S/O LATE M SHANKAR RAO AGED ABOUT 52 YEARS CLOTH MERCHANT R/O B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE TOWN, TARIKER TALUK. CHICKMAGALORE DISTRICT-577101. ... APPELLANT (By Sri: M SHIVAPPA, SR. ADV. ) AND 1. M S NAGARAJ SINCE DEAD BY LRS (1a) NAGARATNA BAI W/O M S NAGARAJ AGED ABOUT 57 YEARS R/O B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. (1b) NAGAVENI D/O M S NAGARAJ AGED ABOUT 27 YEARS 2 R/O B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101 (1c) GAYATRI D/O M S NAGARAJ AGED ABOUT 25 YEARS R/O B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. (1d) SHANKARAMURTHY S/O M S NAGARAJ AGED ABOUT 23 YEARS R/O B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101 2. M S CHANDRAKANTH S/O LATE M SHANKAR RAO DEAD BY LR's a) SMT. SAROJA BAI W/O LATE CHANDRAKANTH AGED ABOUT 54 YEARS b) M.C. DEVARAJ S/O LATE CHANDRAKANTH AGED ABOUT 30 YEARS BOTH ARE R/O C/O AMBICA WATCH WORKS SANTHEPETE CHITRADURGA (AMENDED AS PER COURT ORDER DT. 21.1.09) 3. S SATHYANARAYANA 3 S/O LATE M SHANKAR RAO AGED ABOUT 48 YEARS CLOTHE MERCHANT, SANTHOSH TEXTILES B.H.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 4. M S YALLOJI RAO S/O LATE M SHANKAR RAO AGED ABOUT 46 YEARS C/O GANESHA TEXTILES, B.H.ROAD TARIKERE, CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 5. M S NARASIMHA MURTHY AGED ABOUT 34 YEARS S/O LATE M SHANKAR RAO SHANKAR ELECTRONICS M.G.ROAD, TARIKERE CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 6. K R NEELAKANTAPPA S/O REVANNASIDDAPPA AGED ABOUT 48 YEARS R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE, TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 7. SHIVAMURTHY S/O GANGAPPA AGED ABOUT 51 YEARS R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 8. SOMASHEKARAPPA S/O RUDREGOWDA AGED ABOUT 53 YEARS 4 R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. 9. B MAHALINGAPPA S/O BASAPPA AGED ABOUT 54 YEARS R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST 10. K S LOKESHAPPA S/O NANJUNDAPPA AGED ABOUT 50 YEARS R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST 11. R GANGADHARAPPA S/O RUDRAPPA AGED ABOUT 53 YEARS R/O DUGLAPURA VILLAGE TARIKERE TALUK CHICKMAGALORE DIST-577101. ... RESPONDENTS (By Sri: A.S. MAHADEVA SWAMY, ADV. SRI: S.R. SREEPRASAD, ADV. R6 TO R11 SRI: B.K. MANJUNATH, ADV FOR R1(D), R3 & R5 NOTICE ARE SERVED TO R1(A), R1(C) R2(A,B) & R4) RSA FILED U/SEC 100 CPC AGAINST THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED: 8.11.2004 PASSED IN RA.NO. 30/1998 ON THE FILE OF THE PRESIDING OFFICER, FAST TRACK COURT, TARIKERE, DISMISSING THE APPEAL AND CONFIRMING THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED: 26.9.1998 PASSED 5 IN OS.NO. 65/1994 ON THE FILE OF THE CIVIL JUDGE (JR.DN.), TRARIKERE. THIS APPEAL HAVING BEEN HEARD AND RESERVED FOR PRONOUNCEMENT OF JUDGMENT, THIS DAY A.V.CHANDRASHEKARA, J., MADE THE FOLLOWING: JUDGMENT The lone plaintiff of an original suit bearing O.S.No.65/1994 which was pending on the file of Court of Civil Judge (Jr.Dn.), Tarikere has filed this appeal under Section 100 of CPC, being aggrieved by the dismissal of his suit filed for relief of partition and separate possession and confirmation of the same by the Court of the FTC, Tarikere in R.A.30/1998. Respondents herein are the defendant Nos.1 to 11 in the said suit. 2. During the pendency of the suit, Nagaraj defendant No.1 died and his legal representatives were brought on record. Defendant Nos.6 to 11 are stated to 6 be the purchasers of the suit schedule property. Parties will be referred to as per ranking before the Trial Court. 3. Plaintiff, defendant No.1 to 5 are the sons of one late Shankar Rao. As described in schedule appended to the plaint, item Nos.(a) to (f) were the joint family properties of the plaintiff and defendant Nos.1 to 5. Originally, the lands stood in the name of Late Shankar Rao. According to the plaintiff, joint family properties were divided through a registered partition deed dated 25.6.1986 and by mistake and by oversight, properties as described in the schedule appended to this plaint were left out in the said partition. It is his case that partition dated 25.6.1986 evidenced through registered partition deed is unjust and unfair and therefore he has filed separate suit in O. S.106 /1989 against defendant Nos.1 to 5 for reopening of the said partition. Plaintiff is stated to have 1/6th share in the properties described in item Nos.(1) to (f). It is his case 7 that defendant Nos.1, 3 & 5 have sold the properties to defendant Nos.6 and 11 through separate sale deeds dated 23.3.1991 and they do not bind him. He is stated to have come to know of this of late after obtaining copies of the sale deeds. Hence, he got issued legal notice dated 13.10.1983. With these pleadings, he had requested the Court to grant 1/6th share and divide the properties by metes and bounds. 4. Deceased defendant No.1 chose to file a detailed written statement admitting the relationship of the parties and the properties being the joint family property at one point of time. According to him, partition deed dated 25.6.1986 is an out and out partition executed between the plaintiff and defendant Nos.1 to 5 out of their volition and it has been acted upon by all the parties. According to defendant No.1, suit filed in O. S.106 /1989 i.e., suit filed for reopening of partition dated 25.6.1986 has been dismissed. This 8 suit is stated to be not maintainable without including this property in the earlier suit. According to the defendants, suit is stated to be one for partial partition and therefore bad in law. It is the case of defendant No.1 that plaintiff and defendant Nos.1 to 3 have sold the land in Sy.No.7 measuring 4 acres for a sum of Rs.23,000/- as per the agreement of sale dated 12.11.1981 executed in favour of Halappa by receiving a sum of Rs.10,000/- as advance. It is further averred that a sum of Rs.1,500/- were received from defendant Nos.1 to 3 as per endorsement dated 21.3.1983 and in all they had received a sum of Rs.11,500/-. Out of the said amount, property allotted to the plaintiff was purchased for Rs.35,000/- and plaintiff has acted upon the partition deed. It is stated that defendant Nos.6 to 11 have purchased properties being the close relatives of agreement holder Halappa and they have converted the same into sites and built houses and that they have already improved the land by planting fruit yielding 9 trees. Since 1981, defendant Nos.6 to 11 are in possession of the properties. Claim of the plaintiff is stated to be barred by law. Hence, he had requested the Court to dismiss the suit. 5. On the basis of the above pleadings, following issues came to be framed: I. Whether the plaintiff proves that the suit schedule properties are the joint family properties? II. Whether the plaintiff proves that the partition dated 25.06.1986 is a partial partition? III. Whether the plaintiff proves that he was defrauded by defendant No. 1 to5 in the partition of 1986 as contended in para 3 of the plaint? IV. Whether the plaintiff is entitled for 1/6th share in the schedule properties? V. Whether the suit is bad for non-joinder of necessary parties as contended in para 1 of written statement? VI. Whether the defendants prove that the partition was acted upon and the partition of 1986 is full and complete partition? VII. Whether the claim is barred by limitation? 10 VIII. Whether the defendants prove that they have perfected title by adverse possession? IX. Whether the Court fee paid is correct? X. Whether this Court has no pecuniary jurisdiction to try the case? XI. Whether the plaintiff is entitled for relief of accounting are claimed? XII. What reliefs parties are entitled? XIII. What Order or Decree? Plaintiff is examined as PW-1 and defendant No.10, defendant No.2 and agreement holder Halappa are examined as DWs-1 to 3 respectively. 18 exhibits have been marked on behalf of the plaintiff and 6 documents have been marked on behalf of the defendants. 6. Ultimately, issues 6 to 8 and 9 have been answered in the affirmative, issue Nos.1 to 5, 10 and 11 in the negative and consequently suit is dismissed after contest vide order dated 26.9.1998. Against the said judgment and decree, a regular appeal was filed under Section 96 of CPC before the Court of Senior Civil 11 Judge, Chikmagalur and later it was withdrawn and transferred to FTC, Tarikere and numbered as R.A.30/1998. The said appeal has been dismissed on 8.11.2004 after contest by framing following points for consideration: I. Whether the appellant has proved that the schedule properties were left out by mistake or by over sight in the partition of 1986? II. Whether the appellant has proved that he is entitled for a share in all the properties described in schedules 'A' to 'F'? III. Whether the impugned Judgment and decree of the Trial Court needs interference? IV. What Order? 7. The learned Judge of the First Appellate Court has answered all the points in the negative and ultimately the said appeal has been dismissed on by confirming the judgment of the trial court. It is these 12 concurrent findings which are called in question on various grounds as set out in the appeal memo. 8. The appeal has been admitted on 22.4.2015 to consider the following substantial questions of law: "Whether both the courts have committed a serious error and illegality in holding that the suit is one for partial partition and therefore the impugned judgment is not maintainable either in law or on facts?" 9. The present appellant was plaintiff in the suit filed in O. S.106 / 99 which was pending on the same court, i.e. Court of Munsiff at Tarikere. The said suit,O. S.106 / 89 was also for partition and separate possession of as many as 12 immovable properties as described in the schedule appended to the said suit. The main ground for seeking partition in the said suit was that the partition evinced through a registered document dated 25.6.1986 was the outcome of misrepresentation of facts and fraud being played upon 13 him. Anyhow burden was on the plaintiff to discharge the initial responsibility of proving the allegations relating to misrepresentation of facts and fraud being played upon him. 10. The appellant chose to file the suit in O. S.106 / 89 for partition and separate possession in respect of 12 items seeking equitable allotment. The plaint was presented on 17.6.1989. It was contested by the defendants therein by filing a detailed written statement on 5.4.1990 and 7th defendant chose to file on 19.3.1990. Plaintiff was examined as PW-1. The said suit was disposed of on 22.11.1994 by which time plaintiff had filed the suit in O. S.65 / 94 seeking share in respect of 6 items of lands in Survey No.7/P of Duglapura village against his brothers, i.e. defendants 1 to 5 and the purchasers, i.e. defendants 6 to 11. The said suit was filed on 15.4.1994 and the 1st defendant therein chose to file written statement on 19.7.1995. 14 O. S.65 / 94 was dismissed vide order dated 26.9.1998. By the time O. S.65 / 94 was filed before the same Munsiff Court at Tarikere seeking partition and separate possession of 1/6th share in respect of 6 items of properties in Survey No.7, the suit, O. S.106 / 89 filed by him for partition and separate possession of 12 items was already pending. 11. He has furnished copies of the sale deeds executed by defendants 1, 3 and 5 in favour of defendants 6 to 11 and they have been marked as Exs.P4 to P10. As could be seen from the certified copies of the sale deeds executed in favour of defendants 6 to 11, they were all obtained on 19.4.1993, long prior to the filing of the suit in O. S.65 /1994. Apart from this, he had issued a legal notice calling upon him to give 1/6th share in Survey No.7/P measuring in all 4 acres in 1993 itself. A copy of the legal notice is marked as Ex.P-11 and copy of the 15 reply got issued by the defendants dated 7.10.1993 is marked at Ex.P12. 12. When the very suit for partition and separate possession of all joint family properties was pending in O. S.106 / 89 , it is ununderstandable as to why this plaintiff did not choose to make an application seeking partition and separate possession in respect of 4 acres in Survey No.7/P also. In fact nothing had come in his way to include these items also in the schedule and also to assign reasons as to why those properties need to be included in the suit. Therefore, both the courts below have come to a conclusion that suit for partial partition is not maintainable either in law or on facts. Both the courts have specifically held that by the time O. S.65 /1994 was filed, the suit in O. S.106 / 89 was pending and by the time 1st defendant chose to file written statement on 19.7.1995, O. S.106 / 89 had already been dismissed after contest. 16 13. Learned senior counsel, Mr.Shivappa has contended that O. S.106 / 89 was dismissed and R.A.13/95 before the Senior Civil Judge, Tarikere, which was later transferred to the Fast Track Court, Tarikere, was also dismissed on 17.8.1995. The finding given in O. S.106 / 89 and R.A.13/95 has been affirmed by this court in R.S.A.1257/04 holding that the plaintiff therein, Vishwanath Rao has thoroughly failed to prove that the earlier partition deed dated 25.6.1986 is the outcome of misrepresentation of facts and fraud being played upon him. 14. Partition may be either total or partial. Partition may be partial as regards persons making it or the property divided. It is open to the members of the joint family to severe interest in respect of a property of the joint estate while retaining their status of joint family and holding the rest as properties of the undivided family. A decision rendered by this court in 17 the case of P.CHARADAPPA PAI .v. AGRICULTURAL INCOME TAX (AIR 1970 Mys.168) is relevant in this regard. 15. Every suit for partition should ordinarily embrace all joint family properties. But a suit for partial partition will lie only when the portion omitted is not in the possession of coparceners and will subsequently be deemed not available for partition. For instance, where a part of the joint family property is in possession of a mortgagee or lessee or is impartible zamindary or being held by strangers who have no interest in the family partition. 16. In the present case, Survey No.7 originally measured in all 14.2 acres out of which 10.12 acres had been acquired during the lifetime of Shankar Rao (father of the plaintiff). The remaining 4 acres was agreed to be sold to Halappa by all the members of the joint family on 12.11.1981 for a total consideration of Rs.23,500/-. 18 Ex.D-6 is the agreement of sale produced by Halappa who is examined as DW-3. It has been executed by Nagaraj, Chandrakant, Vishwanath (plaintiff) and another person by name Satyanarayana. The scribe of the document is N.S.Nagappa who is very well known to the plaintiff. Ex.D-6 was confronted to PW-1 and got marked on behalf of the defendants. He has admitted the signatures of his brothers-Nagaraj, Chandrakant and Satyanarayana found in Ex.D-6. He has also identified the signature of Nagappa, the scribe of Ex.D- 6. Of course PW-1 has denied his signature on Ex.D-6 when confronted to him. Any how some useful admissions are elicited from his mouth to demonstrate that the scribe of Ex.D-6 was very well known to the plaintiff and his family members and that he had written many documents of the family.. 17. It is true that there is no reference in Ex.D-6 about possession of the property being handed over to 19 the agreement holder-Nagappa. But in the subsequent endorsement there is a specific mention about the possession being handed over while receiving additional evidence. But in all the sale deeds executed in favour of defendants 6 to 11 vide Exs.D1 to D5, there is a specific reference that the purchasers were already in possession of their respective extent of lands sold in their favour. For example, in Ex.D-1, registered sale deed executed in favour of Lokesh by defendants 1, 3 and 5, there is a specific reference that 20 guntas of land sold in his favour was already in his possession. The said land was sold for Rs.5,500/-. Similarly an extent of 1.39 acres was sold in favour of Shivashankarappa for Rs.3,000/- and there is a reference that the land in question was already in his possession. Similar reference is found in Ex.D-3 executed in favour of Shivamurthy relating to 39 guntas of land. Similar reference is found in Ex.D-4 executed in favour of Gangappa by defendants 1, 3 and 5 relating to 20 30 guntas of land for Rs.4,500/-. Similar reference is found in Ex.D-5, sale deed executed in favour of Mahalingappa by defendants 1, 3 and 5 relating to 30 guntas of land for Rs.6,000/-. 18. It is not as though the joint family had innumerable properties and the location of the same could not have been remembered by the coparceners. In fact the joint family consisted of 6-7 persons and comprised only 13 items and a suit had already been filed seeking equitable allotment by re-opening the partition deed dated 25.6.1986. In O. S.106 / 89 , nothing came in the way of the plaintiff to have requested the Court to include these items also in that suit. 19. By the time the deposition of PW-1 was recorded in O. S.65 / 94 , the suit in O. S.106 / 89 had already been dismissed and appeal was pending before the Senior Civil Judge at Tarikere. PW1 has admitted to that effect in page 2 of his cross-examination recorded 21 on 20.1.1998. He has further admitted that himself, his mother and brothers divided the joint family properties o 25.6.1986 through a registered partition deed and the sharers have been enjoying their respective shares according to the allotment made. He has further admitted that a few sharers have already alienated their shares after the partition. He has further admitted that the land measuring 4 acres of Duglapura village had not been included in 1986 partition deed. He has feigned ignorance as to what the 6th defendant is doing with the land purchased by him relating to Survey No.7. He has admitted that the schedule land in No.7/P is attached to the road in Dugalapura village. He has feigned ignorance about the suggestion that the entire 4 acres had been agreed to be sold in favour of Halappa and that the said Halappa got sale deeds in favour of defendants 6 to 11. He has further feigned ignorance about a suggestion put to him that the purchasers have 22 already converted the lands into sites and have been using the same. 20. He has admitted that at the time of partition in June 1986, the family business was being run under the name and style of Shankar Rao & Sons at Tarikere and accounts of the said shop was being looked after by the 2nd defendant-Chandrakant. PW-1 has further admitted that he has taken over the said business and it was earlier called as Mahendrakar Cloth Centre. He has admitted that he is now running the said textile business in the same property apart from running another business called as Vijayasri Textiles owned by him since 1.4.1997. 21. A suit for partial partition will be maintainable provided the family property was outside the family when the other properties were divided, or when the title relating to a land granted to the family was in dispute before the land grant authorities. After 23 the conclusion of litigation in relation of land grant, the same could be divided as it would not be available for partition when the other joint family properties are divided. 22. A division Bench of this court in the case of G.M.MAHENDRA .v. G.M.MOHAN & ANOTHER (2011(4) KCCR 2461) has reiterated that Order II Rule 2, C.P.C . would be applicable in cases where a suit for partition is filed by non-alienating coparceners. It is further held that non-including of purchasers will deny them the right of seeking equitable allotment. 23. In the present case, the plaintiff had already filed a suit in O. S.106 / 89 seeking partition and separate possession of 12 immovable properties and it was pending for more than 5 years. During the pendency of the said suit, he chose to file another suit in respect of 4 acres in Survey No.7/P against his brothers and purchasers. If the plaintiff had included all 24 these properties in the earlier suit and had made the purchasers as parties in the said suit, the purchasers could have requested the court to make an equitable allotment and thus their interest could be safeguarded out of the share that would be allotted to their vendors. That opportunity is presently denied to defendants 6 to 11 herein who are purchasers of the suit schedule property. 24. On 12.5.1981, only a sum of Rs.10,000/- was received out of the agreed consideration of Rs.23,500/-. On 21.7.1983, Chandrakant andNagaraj chose to receive another Rs.2,000/- from Halappa and handed over possession of the suit schedule property. As such the property in question measuring 4 acres was in possession of Halappa. Defendants 6 to 11 are close relatives of Halappa who have purchased in bits. In this regard there is reference in all the sale deeds that the purchasers were in possession of the extent of land 25 purchased by them. Though the plaintiff has denied the authenticity of Ex.D-6, it could be said that it has been proved. The scribe of Ex.D-1, Nagappa was very well known to the plaintiff and he had written many documents for the family. Therefore the signature of the plaintiff found in Ex.D-6 cannot be doubted. 25. If really 4 acres of land I Survey No.7/P was in possession of the family, he would have definitely included them when he filed the suit in O. S.106 / 89 . The very fact that he chose to file the suit in 1994, three years after the alienation was made, would speak in volumes against the plaintiff. As already stated, defendants 6 to 11-purchasers have been denied of the opportunity to seek equitable partition since these properties were not included in the earlier suit and they were not made parties. PW-1 has specifically admitted that many sharers have already sold their respective share allotted to them on 25.6.1986. 26 26. Thus viewed from any angle, both the courts have properly analyzed the oral and documentary evidence in right perspective keeping in mind the admissions elicited from the mouth of PW-1 and circumstances under which the subsequent suit was filed for partition and separate possession. Thus both the courts have tested the oral and documentary evidence on the touchstone of intrinsic probabilities and have adopted right approach to the real state of affairs. Thus the substantial question of law is to be answered in the affirmative and consequently the appeal will have to be dismissed. 27. In the result the following order is passed: ORDER Appeal filed under Section 100 , C.P.C. challenging the judgment passed in R.A.30/98 (arising out of O.S. No.65/1994 which was pending on the file of Presiding 27 Officer, Fast Track Court, Tarikere, is dismissed. Consequently the judgments of both the courts are upheld. Parties to bear their own costs. Sd/- JUDGE DM/vgh*
